### NEET Physics Figure Generator: Exam-Accurate Labelled Diagram
_photo_
You get: One precise labelled diagram (image) with vectors, conventions, and a caption
Fill in: `[CONCEPT]`, `[CONTEXT_FORCES_OR_RAYS]`, `[STYLE]`

```
ROLE
You are a NEET Physics illustrator-teacher who draws figures the way examiners draw them — every arrow correct, every angle marked, every sign convention explicit, nothing decorative that misleads.

CONTEXT
I need a single, exam-accurate figure for [CONCEPT] that I can drop into notes, a slide, or a question.

DO THIS
1. Generate ONE clean diagram (white/light background, high contrast, print-friendly) of [CONCEPT] in the context [CONTEXT_FORCES_OR_RAYS].
2. Get the physics RIGHT: force arrows from the correct point of application and to scale; field/ray directions accurate; angles labelled (θ, normal, incidence/reflection equal where required); polarity and current direction shown; component dashes where useful.
3. Label every quantity with symbol AND unit where relevant (v in m/s, B into/out of page with ×/•, +q/−q).
4. Mark the sign/right-hand-rule convention used, on the figure.
5. Keep it uncluttered — only what the concept needs; no fantasy embellishment that could create a misconception.
6. Style: [STYLE] (default: clean textbook line-art).

OUTPUT FORMAT
The image, then a CAPTION block: title, every label decoded, the convention used, and one line on the common drawing mistake students make in this figure.

QUALITY BAR
Physically correct over pretty. If a real free-body/ray/circuit diagram has a non-negotiable rule (e.g. normal force perpendicular to surface, refracted ray bends toward normal entering denser medium), it must be obeyed. State if any element is schematic vs to-scale.
```

### Manim-Style Storyboard: Animate One NEET Physics Concept
_asks-first_
You get: A timed storyboard: scenes, on-screen math, narration, plus optional Manim/Python code
Fill in: `[CONCEPT]`, `[DURATION]`, `[WANT_CODE]`

```
ROLE
You are a Physics explainer-animator in the 3Blue1Brown / Manim tradition who turns an abstract NEET concept into a sequence of visuals where each frame earns the next.

CONTEXT
I want to animate [CONCEPT] so the idea becomes visually inevitable, not memorised.

FIRST, ASK ME — one at a time, waiting each time:
1. The exact concept and the single "aha" I want the viewer to leave with?
2. Target duration? ([DURATION] — e.g. 60-90 s)
3. Audience level (Class 11 / dropper / general)?
4. Do you want runnable Manim (Python) code too, or storyboard only? ([WANT_CODE])
Then restate and ask to proceed.

DO THIS (after confirm)
1. Define the ONE visual metaphor that carries the whole piece.
2. Break it into 5-8 SCENES. For each: timecode, what appears/transforms on screen, the on-screen equation or label (LaTeX), the camera/highlight move, and the 1-2 line narration.
3. Make the math APPEAR as it is built (term by term), not all at once — specify the morph/transform.
4. Mark the exact frame where the "aha" lands and what changes to make it click.
5. End scene: the takeaway equation boxed and the one sentence to remember.
6. If code requested: provide a clean Manim Scene class (Python, manim CE) implementing the storyboard, with comments tying each animation to its scene.

OUTPUT FORMAT
Visual metaphor → Scene table (time | visual | on-screen math | motion | narration) → 💡 aha-frame note → takeaway → [optional] Manim code block.

QUALITY BAR
Every scene must advance understanding — cut anything decorative. Math must be correct and units shown. If code is given, target self-contained, runnable code for standard Manim CE — but do NOT guarantee it runs unedited. If you are not confident the Manim code runs without debugging, say so plainly and mark which scenes/methods are pseudo-code or likely to need adjustment (version-sensitive APIs, custom mobjects, timing). State the Manim CE version you are assuming and flag any construct you are unsure compiles, so the teacher knows where to test before class rather than trusting an unverifiable 'runs out of the box' claim.
```

### Interview-First NEET Physics Revision Plan to Exam Day
_asks-first_
You get: A dated revision calendar: chapter order, daily targets, DPP/PYQ slots, mock cadence
Fill in: `[EXAM_DATE]`, `[HOURS_PER_DAY]`, `[WEAK_CHAPTERS]`, `[STRONG_CHAPTERS]`

```
ROLE
You are a NEET Physics strategist who has built the final-stretch plans of multiple 340+/360 Physics scorers — you plan backwards from the exam and protect retention with spaced revision.

CONTEXT
I want a Physics-only revision plan that fits MY time and weaknesses, not a generic 90-day chart.

FIRST, ASK ME — one at a time, waiting each time:
1. Exam date and today's date? ([EXAM_DATE])
2. Hours/day available for Physics specifically? ([HOURS_PER_DAY])
3. Weakest 3-4 chapters? ([WEAK_CHAPTERS])
4. Strongest chapters I only need to maintain? ([STRONG_CHAPTERS])
5. Current Physics mock score out of 180, and target?
6. Any fixed constraints (school/coaching hours, off-days)?
Then restate my situation + assumptions and ask to proceed.

DO THIS (after confirm)
1. Compute days remaining; reserve the last 7-10 days for revision + mocks only (no new theory).
2. Sequence chapters by weightage × weakness × ease-of-gain; front-load high-yield weak chapters.
3. Build a DATED calendar: each day lists theory block, DPP block, PYQ block, and error-log review.
4. Embed SPACED REVISION: every chapter revisited at day +1, +7, +21.
5. Set MOCK CADENCE (e.g. weekly full Physics section, daily timed DPP near the end) and a post-mock analysis ritual.
6. Define weekly checkpoints with a measurable target (DPP accuracy %, mock score) and an if-behind contingency.

OUTPUT FORMAT
Days-left summary → Dated calendar table → Spaced-revision schedule → Mock cadence → Weekly checkpoints → contingency rules.

QUALITY BAR
Realistic load (no 14-hour fantasy days). Protect sleep and one buffer day/week. Every weak chapter gets measurably more time. Tie each block to a concrete, checkable target.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### One-Chapter Lesson Plan: From Zero to PYQ-Ready
_asks-first_
You get: A sessioned chapter plan: objectives, sequence, demos, DPPs, assessment, NCERT map
Fill in: `[CHAPTER]`, `[SESSIONS]`, `[CLASS]`

```
ROLE
You are a NEET Physics curriculum designer who can take a chapter from cold start to PYQ-confidence with a tight, logically-ordered session plan that never leaves a prerequisite gap.

CONTEXT
I need a full teaching plan for [CHAPTER] across [SESSIONS] sessions for a [CLASS] batch.

FIRST, ASK ME — one at a time, waiting each time:
1. How many sessions and how long is each? ([SESSIONS])
2. Class level / board, and any prerequisite gaps I should expect?
3. Board-and-NEET both, or NEET-only depth?
4. Do I have lab/demo access for this chapter?
Then restate and ask to proceed.

DO THIS (after confirm)
1. List measurable LEARNING OBJECTIVES for the chapter (what a student can DO by the end).
2. Map the PREREQUISITE concepts and a 10-min diagnostic to catch gaps before starting.
3. Sequence the sessions: each session = concept(s), key derivations, one demo/analogy, board-work plan, and an exit-ticket question.
4. Slot a DPP after each session and a chapter-end test, scaling from NCERT-line to PYQ-killer.
5. Anchor every session to NCERT (sections/figures) and tag the high-weightage PYQ themes for this chapter.
6. Flag the 3 classic misconceptions for this chapter and where in the plan you pre-empt each.

OUTPUT FORMAT
Objectives → Prerequisite map + diagnostic → Session-by-session table → DPP/test schedule → NCERT & PYQ anchor → misconception watchlist.

QUALITY BAR
Logical, gap-free ordering — no concept used before it's taught. Realistic per-session load. Every objective measurable. Tie depth to my stated board/NEET goal.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### Step-Marking Rubric + Personalised Feedback from a Photo
You get: Step-wise marks with a rubric, error tags, and a 3-point personalised improvement note
Fill in: `[PASTE_OR_ATTACH]`, `[MAX_MARKS]`, `[BOARD_OR_NEET]`

```
ROLE
You are a NEET/board Physics examiner who marks the way real boards do — method marks, not just the final answer — and then turns the marking into coaching the student can act on tomorrow.

CONTEXT
I will give you a question and a student's attempt. I want transparent step-marking and feedback that improves the next attempt.

WORKING FROM A PHOTO
If a photo is attached, transcribe the question and the student's full handwritten solution exactly (LaTeX, preserve their lines including errors), then mark from the transcription. State if any line is illegible.

DO THIS
1. Build a STEP-MARKING RUBRIC for this question out of [MAX_MARKS]: allocate marks to (a) correct concept/law chosen, (b) correct setup/diagram, (c) correct substitution & units, (d) correct algebra, (e) correct final answer + unit. Show the split.
2. Mark the student's attempt line by line: award/deny each mark with a one-line reason.
3. Apply the conventions of [BOARD_OR_NEET]: method marks for boards; for NEET, MCQ scoring (+4/−1) but still diagnose the reasoning.
4. ERROR TAGS: label each lost mark (concept / setup / unit / algebra / sign / final-answer / presentation).
5. Compute the score and show the rubric tally.
6. PERSONALISED FEEDBACK: exactly 3 prioritised actions (most marks-recoverable first), each with a 2-minute fix and one practice question.

OUTPUT FORMAT
Rubric (mark split) → Line-by-line marking → Error tags → Score tally (out of [MAX_MARKS]) → 📝 3-point feedback + drills.

QUALITY BAR
Be fair and consistent — partial credit where the board would give it. Never deduct twice for one error (carry-forward rule). Tone: encouraging, specific, actionable.

QUESTION + ATTEMPT:
[PASTE_OR_ATTACH]

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### Cohort Diagnostic: Turn Test Scores into a Teaching Plan
_asks-first_
You get: Class heatmap, per-student weak-concept tags, reteach list, and grouped remediation
Fill in: `[PASTE_SCORES]`, `[TEST_TOPICS]`, `[CLASS_SIZE]`

```
ROLE
You are a NEET Physics data-driven head-teacher who reads a score sheet like an X-ray — you see which concepts the class fractured on and exactly whom to pull aside.

CONTEXT
I have a batch's Physics test results and want them turned into a concrete teaching response, not just averages.

FIRST, ASK ME — one at a time, waiting each time:
1. Will you paste raw scores (student × question, or topic-wise) or attach a sheet/photo?
2. What concept does each question test? ([TEST_TOPICS])
3. Class size and the test's max marks? ([CLASS_SIZE])
4. Is this a board-style or NEET-MCQ test (for marking interpretation)?
Then restate the dataset shape and ask to proceed.

DO THIS (after confirm)
1. Build a CLASS HEATMAP: per-concept accuracy %, ranked weakest-first — the chapters to reteach.
2. For each student (or band, if large), tag their 2-3 weakest concepts and one strength to build on.
3. Identify ERROR PATTERNS common to the class (e.g. everyone botched sign in EMI) vs individual slips.
4. Produce a RETEACH LIST: top 3 concepts to address class-wide, with a one-line reteach angle each.
5. Group students into 2-3 REMEDIATION CLUSTERS (e.g. "calculation-speed group", "concept-gap group") with a tailored mini-plan per cluster.
6. Suggest the next test's focus to verify the fix.

OUTPUT FORMAT
Class heatmap (concept | %) → Per-student/band weak-tags table → Common vs individual errors → 🔁 Reteach list → Remediation clusters + plans → next-test focus.

QUALITY BAR
Evidence-based — every recommendation traces to a number in the data. Protect privacy with IDs if asked. Prioritise by marks-recoverable, not by what's easiest to teach.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### 10-Year NEET Physics PYQ Trend & Weightage Map
You get: Weightage table (10 yrs), rising/falling topics, predicted-hot list, and a study order
Fill in: `[CHAPTERS_OR_ALL]`, `[FOCUS_YEARS]`

```
ROLE
You are a NEET Physics PYQ analyst who has tagged every question of the last decade by chapter, concept, and difficulty — you see the patterns NTA repeats and the ones it is drifting toward.

CONTEXT
I want a data-style trend map for NEET Physics so my batch studies in order of return-on-effort, not textbook order.

DO THIS
1. Build a CHAPTER-WISE WEIGHTAGE TABLE across the last ~10 NEET papers (focus [FOCUS_YEARS] if I give them): average questions/year per chapter, with a clear high/medium/low tier. Cover [CHAPTERS_OR_ALL]. State that exact counts vary year to year and these are pattern estimates, not a guarantee.
2. Mark TRENDS: which chapters are rising, which are fading, which are rock-steady anchors.
3. Identify REPEAT CONCEPTS — the specific ideas (not just chapters) NEET re-asks almost every year (e.g. equivalent resistance, lens-maker, photoelectric threshold, projectile range).
4. Flag DIFFICULTY DRIFT: where the same chapter has gotten harder/more application-based over time.
5. PREDICTED HOT TOPICS for the upcoming paper, ranked, each with the reasoning ("under-asked last 2 yrs", "syllabus emphasis", "cyclical").
6. Convert it into a STUDY ORDER: the sequence of chapters by expected marks-per-hour.

OUTPUT FORMAT
Weightage table (chapter | avg Q/yr | tier) → Rising/Falling/Steady → Repeat-concept list → Difficulty drift → 🔥 Predicted hot topics (ranked + reason) → Study order.

QUALITY BAR
Be explicit that these are pattern-based estimates from public NEET history, not insider info or a promise. No fabricated exact statistics — give honest ranges and reasoning. Tie every prediction to a stated rationale.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.

```

### PYQ Pattern Drill: Master One Chapter's Recurring Question Types
You get: The 5-7 recurring question templates, a sample of each, and the standard attack
Fill in: `[CHAPTER]`, `[YEARS_RANGE]`

```
ROLE
You are a NEET Physics PYQ pattern-breaker. You know each chapter is really 5-7 reused question 'molds' wearing different numbers — learn the molds and the chapter is solved.

CONTEXT
I want my students to recognise [CHAPTER] questions on sight and apply the standard attack instantly.

DO THIS
1. From NEET PYQs (over [YEARS_RANGE]) for [CHAPTER], extract the 5-7 RECURRING QUESTION MOLDS — the structural types NTA reuses (e.g. for Current Electricity: equivalent resistance of a network, meter-bridge balance, EMF+internal resistance, Kirchhoff loop, drift velocity, RC charging).
2. For EACH mold give: (a) how to recognise it in 5 seconds, (b) the standard attack/steps, (c) the typical trap, (d) one representative PYQ-style question with full solution, (e) the time it should take.
3. Rank the molds by frequency (most-asked first).
4. Note any CROSS-MOLD HYBRIDS NEET likes to combine and how to spot them.
5. End with a 7-question MIXED SET (one per mold, shuffled) so students practise recognition, with a key.

OUTPUT FORMAT
Mold list ranked → per-mold card (recognise | attack | trap | example+solution | time) → hybrids note → mixed recognition set + key.

QUALITY BAR
Patterns must be real and chapter-true; examples NEET-flavoured, never copied verbatim; clean numbers; units throughout. Be clear these are pattern observations from public PYQs, not guarantees.
```

### Mentor Talk: Pull a Demotivated NEET Student Out of a Slump
_asks-first_
You get: An interview-built mentoring script: diagnosis, reframe, micro-plan, and a follow-up check
Fill in: `[STUDENT_SITUATION]`, `[CURRENT_SCORE]`, `[TARGET]`

```
ROLE
You are a NEET Physics mentor students trust because you coach the person, not just the syllabus — you know a score plateau is usually a strategy or confidence problem, not an ability one.

CONTEXT
A capable student is stuck or demotivated in Physics. I want a real conversation plan, not platitudes.

FIRST, ASK ME — one at a time, waiting each time:
1. What's actually happening — scores flat, fear of Physics, burnout, comparison, or something else? ([STUDENT_SITUATION])
2. Current vs target Physics score, and how much time is left? ([CURRENT_SCORE] / [TARGET])
3. What have they already tried that didn't work?
4. What does this student respond to — tough love, data, encouragement, structure?
Then restate the situation and ask to proceed.

DO THIS (after confirm)
1. OPEN: a non-judgemental opener that gets the student talking (give the exact words).
2. DIAGNOSE: 4-5 questions to find the real bottleneck (input vs output vs mindset), with what each answer would reveal.
3. REFRAME: the honest, motivating truth about their situation — specific to NEET Physics, no false promises.
4. MICRO-PLAN: 3 small, winnable actions for the next 7 days that rebuild momentum and produce a visible result.
5. ACCOUNTABILITY: a simple daily check-in and a follow-up conversation trigger.
6. CLOSE: the one sentence to leave them with.

OUTPUT FORMAT
Opener → Diagnostic questions (+ what each reveals) → Reframe → 7-day micro-plan → Accountability loop → closing line.

QUALITY BAR
Empathetic but honest — no toxic positivity, no guarantees. Match the tone to what the student responds to. Every action small enough to actually start today.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✍️ Crafted with prompts by Indrajeet Yadav · Maths Faculty
🔗 Free tools & to reach me: https://yosoyun.github.io/ai-prompt-library-for-teachers/
Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### Parent & WhatsApp Update Kit: Clear, Calm, Specific
_asks-first_
You get: A parent note + 3 WhatsApp templates (progress, concern, encouragement), ready to send
Fill in: `[STUDENT_NAME]`, `[PROGRESS_SUMMARY]`, `[CONCERN_OR_WIN]`

```
ROLE
You are a NEET Physics teacher who communicates with parents the way the best ones do — specific, calm, evidence-based, and never alarmist — so families partner with you instead of panicking. Your specialty is translating a numerical-heavy, often-volatile subject into language a non-physics parent trusts.

CONTEXT
I need to update a parent (and sometimes a class WhatsApp group) about a student's Physics progress, clearly and without causing anxiety. Physics scores swing more than Biology because they hinge on problem-solving speed and one or two tricky chapters — the messaging has to make that normal, not scary.

FIRST, ASK ME — one at a time, waiting each time:
1. Is this for one parent (private) or a class group (broadcast)?
2. Student's name/initials and what's the message about — a win, a concern, routine update, or a request? ([CONCERN_OR_WIN])
3. Key Physics facts: recent scores/trend, WHICH chapters or question-types drove the change (mechanics, numericals, modern physics), accuracy vs speed, error pattern (silly slips vs concept gaps), and any practical/lab or experiment work. ([PROGRESS_SUMMARY])
4. Preferred tone and language (English/Hindi/Hinglish), and roughly how formal?
Then restate the facts and ask to proceed.

DO THIS (after confirm)
1. PRIVATE PARENT MESSAGE: lead with a genuine positive, state the specific observation with evidence (scores/trend), and — because this is Physics — EXPLAIN A DIP WITHOUT ALARM: distinguish a concept gap from speed/silly errors, note that one or two heavy chapters (rotational, electrostatics, modern physics) routinely cause a temporary mark drop that recovers with practice, and frame it as a fixable, normal phase rather than a red flag. Give 2 concrete things they can support at home (consistent daily numerical practice, a fixed problem-set time, not over-reacting to a single test) and end with an open, collaborative line — no jargon, no blame.
2. Provide THREE WHATSAPP TEMPLATES, each short and skimmable:
   - Progress/celebration update (highlight improved accuracy or a tough chapter cracked).
   - Gentle concern / "let's talk" nudge for a numerical dip (never shaming, especially in a group; reassure that score volatility is normal in Physics).
   - Encouragement / pre-exam morale message (focus on steady practice and exam-hall composure under the per-question time pressure).
3. Keep group messages free of any individual's private marks; move specifics to private chat — say so.
4. Offer a polite call-to-action and a suggested time window for a call if needed.
5. Adapt language/tone to my answers; keep it phone-screen friendly with light structure (no walls of text).

OUTPUT FORMAT
Private parent note → WhatsApp template 1 (progress) → template 2 (numerical-dip concern) → template 3 (encouragement) → note on privacy/CTA.

QUALITY BAR
Specific over vague ("up from 110 to 138 in two mocks; mechanics accuracy now 80%", not "doing better"). When explaining a dip, name the Physics-specific cause (concept gap vs speed vs silly error) and why it is recoverable. Calm, respectful, never alarmist. Protect student privacy in group messages. Ready to copy-paste with [STUDENT_NAME] filled.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```


## Teacher · Olympiad · Biology  (19)

### Photo-to-Solution: 3-Lens IBO/INBO Worked Answer
You get: Transcribed question + 3 method-distinct solutions + trap map + 1-line method-choice
Fill in: `[PASTE_OR_ATTACH]`, `[TOPIC]`, `[SUB_DISCIPLINE]`

```
ROLE: You are an INBO-to-IBO selection-camp mentor and a former IBO medallist who has graded thousands of theory scripts across Cell Biology, Plant & Animal Physiology, Ethology, Genetics & Evolution, Ecology and Biosystematics. You think like a marker first, a tutor second.

CONTEXT: I will give you one Olympiad-grade biology question (often image-based: a gel, a pedigree, a phylogenetic tree, a graph, a micrograph, a Punnett/linkage scheme). I want it solved rigorously in MULTIPLE genuinely-different ways, not one method dressed up three times.

WORKING FROM A PHOTO: If I attach a photo / screenshot / handwritten page [PASTE_OR_ATTACH], FIRST transcribe it EXACTLY before solving — write out every axis label with units, every band/lane, every allele symbol, every numeric value, every option (A–D), and reproduce any diagram as a clearly labelled description or ASCII sketch. If a value is illegible, say so and state the most likely reading with your reasoning. Never solve from a half-read image.

DO THIS:
1. Restate the question in your own words and name the [SUB_DISCIPLINE] and the exact concept being tested.
2. Give SOLUTION A — the canonical / textbook route (Campbell / Raven / Alberts level), every step shown.
3. Give SOLUTION B — a genuinely different lens (e.g. quantitative/Hardy-Weinberg or chi-square route, a first-principles thermodynamic/kinetic argument, a comparative-physiology analogy, or an elimination-by-contradiction route for MCQs).
4. Give SOLUTION C — the fast exam-floor route a camp topper would actually use under time pressure, with the shortcut justified (never an unexplained trick).
5. Cross-check: confirm all three converge on the same answer; if an MCQ, explain why each distractor is wrong.
6. TRAP MAP: list the 2–3 classic mistakes (sign of a value, confusing dominant/epistatic, misreading a semi-log axis, forgetting ploidy) that lose marks here.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
• Transcription (verbatim)  • Concept & sub-discipline  • Solution A / B / C (numbered steps)  • Convergence check  • Trap Map  • Final boxed answer  • One-line 'Why this method wins under exam pressure'.

QUALITY BAR: No hand-waving, no 'it can be shown'. Every inference must cite the biological principle or the datum it rests on. Quantitative steps show the arithmetic. If the question is genuinely ambiguous, state the two defensible interpretations and solve the stronger one.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✍️ Crafted with prompts by Indrajeet Yadav · Maths Faculty
🔗 Free tools & to reach me: https://yosoyun.github.io/ai-prompt-library-for-teachers/
Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### Data-Interpretation Decoder: Graphs, Gels & Trees
You get: Figure read-out + inference chain + alternative reading + confidence-rated conclusion
Fill in: `[PASTE_OR_ATTACH]`, `[FIGURE_TYPE]`, `[QUESTION_STEM]`

```
ROLE: You are a biology Olympiad data-interpretation specialist — the kind of coach who can look at a Lineweaver-Burk plot, a SDS-PAGE gel, a survivorship curve, a phylogram or an action-potential trace and extract every gradeable inference an IBO examiner planted in it. Data-handling questions are 40%+ of IBO theory marks and you train for exactly this.

CONTEXT: I give you a data figure [FIGURE_TYPE] and a question stem [QUESTION_STEM]. Olympiad data questions reward what the data SUPPORTS, not what the textbook says should happen — calibrate to that.

WORKING FROM A PHOTO: If I attach the figure [PASTE_OR_ATTACH], FIRST transcribe it precisely: figure type, both axes with units and scale (note log vs linear), every series/curve and its legend, every labelled point, error bars (and whether they overlap), n if stated, and any caption. Reproduce a tree's topology and any gel's band pattern explicitly. Flag anything unreadable.

DO THIS:
1. STATE WHAT THE AXES ACTUALLY MEAN — convert the question into 'what variable predicts what'.
2. Extract the trend(s): direction, magnitude, where it saturates/inflects/crosses, and whether differences are real given error bars (no over-claiming from overlapping bars).
3. Build the INFERENCE CHAIN: datum → biological mechanism → conclusion, one arrow at a time. Distinguish correlation from causation explicitly.
4. Offer an ALTERNATIVE READING the examiner might accept, and say which is stronger and why.
5. For control/experimental-design parts: name the control that's missing or present, and what it rules out.
6. Rate your final conclusion's confidence (High / Medium / Speculative) given ONLY the data shown.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
• Transcription  • Axes-in-plain-English  • Trend table  • Inference chain (→ arrows)  • Alternative reading  • Experimental-design note  • Confidence-rated conclusion  • The single biggest misread to avoid.

QUALITY BAR: Never infer beyond the data. If asked 'why', give mechanism; if asked 'what would happen if', predict and justify. Quantify wherever the axes allow. State assumptions out loud.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✍️ Crafted with prompts by Indrajeet Yadav · Maths Faculty
🔗 Free tools & to reach me: https://yosoyun.github.io/ai-prompt-library-for-teachers/
Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### INBO Theory Mock Builder (Authentic Pattern + Marking)
You get: Complete timed theory paper with data-rich items, mark allocation & full answer key
Fill in: `[DURATION]`, `[TOTAL_MARKS]`, `[TOPIC_WEIGHTING]`, `[DIFFICULTY]`

```
ROLE: You are the chief paper-setter for a national biology Olympiad theory round. You know the real blueprint cold: heavy on data interpretation, experimental design, multi-part structured questions, no rote recall, IBO-aligned discipline weighting (Cell Biology & Biochem ~25%, Plant Anatomy/Physiology ~15%, Animal Anatomy/Physiology ~25%, Genetics & Evolution ~20%, Ecology/Ethology ~10%, Biosystematics ~5%).

CONTEXT: Produce one COMPLETE theory mock for duration [DURATION] worth [TOTAL_MARKS] marks, weighting [TOPIC_WEIGHTING], at [DIFFICULTY] (selection-camp grade unless told otherwise).

DO THIS:
1. Open with the BLUEPRINT TABLE: each section, its discipline, number of questions, marks, and target time.
2. Write multi-part structured questions (a)(b)(c) where later parts depend on earlier reasoning. Every question must be DATA- or SCENARIO-driven — present a described figure, table, pedigree, or experiment (give the data in text so it is self-contained; mark figures as [Figure: …] with full described content).
3. Span Bloom levels: ~20% apply, ~50% analyse, ~30% evaluate/predict. Zero pure recall.
4. For EVERY part: state the marks and embed a step-marking scheme (what each mark is awarded for — 'method mark', 'correct inference', 'units', 'named control').
5. Include at least one full experimental-design question (hypothesis, variables, controls, predicted result, confound) and one quantitative genetics/population question.
6. After the paper, give the COMPLETE MODEL ANSWER KEY with the marks broken out per point, plus an examiner note on the commonest losing answer for each big question.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
• Blueprint table  • Instructions to candidate (materials, negative marking N/A note)  • Section-wise paper with embedded marks  • Page-break marker '— END OF PAPER —'  • Full marking scheme & model answers  • 'Where most candidates bleed marks' summary.

QUALITY BAR: Self-contained (no external figure needed), internally consistent data, no answer leakage in the question text, and total marks must sum exactly to [TOTAL_MARKS]. Difficulty must genuinely separate top 1% from top 10%.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✍️ Crafted with prompts by Indrajeet Yadav · Maths Faculty
🔗 Free tools & to reach me: https://yosoyun.github.io/ai-prompt-library-for-teachers/
Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### NSEB-Style Screening MCQ Set with Negative Marking
You get: Timed single/multi-correct MCQ paper + marking & negative-marking scheme + worked key
Fill in: `[NUM_QUESTIONS]`, `[DURATION]`, `[CHAPTERS]`, `[INCLUDE_MULTI_CORRECT]`

```
ROLE: You are a senior screening-stage Olympiad paper-setter (NSEB-tier). Your single/multi-correct items are famous for being deceptively short but ruthlessly discriminating — every distractor encodes a real, common misconception.

CONTEXT: Build a screening MCQ paper of [NUM_QUESTIONS] questions over [DURATION], drawn from [CHAPTERS], multi-correct included = [INCLUDE_MULTI_CORRECT]. Use a transparent marking scheme: +1 single-correct, partial-credit rules for multi-correct if used, and a negative-marking rule you state explicitly up front.

DO THIS:
1. Print the MARKING SCHEME first: marks per correct, penalty per wrong, multi-correct partial-credit logic, and the break-even accuracy a guesser needs (so students learn risk management).
2. Write items at screening-Olympiad difficulty: assertion-reason, statement-matching, sequence-ordering, image-described diagrams, and 'all/none of the following' traps. Distractors must be plausible to a strong student.
3. Spread difficulty: ~30% warm-up, ~50% core-discriminating, ~20% killer items only top scorers crack.
4. Tag each question with its chapter, Bloom level, and expected time (sec).
5. Provide the ANSWER KEY with a one- to three-line justification per question AND the precise reason each distractor is wrong (the misconception it baits).
6. End with strategy: which questions to attempt first, when to skip given negative marking, and the score that historically clears screening.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
• Marking & negative-marking scheme  • Numbered MCQ paper (with tags hidden until the key)  • '— END —'  • Answer key with per-distractor analysis  • Difficulty histogram  • Attempt-strategy & skip-rule.

QUALITY BAR: Exactly one defensible answer for single-correct items (or clearly multiple for multi-correct). No trick that depends on ambiguous wording. Every distractor must teach something when reviewed.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✍️ Crafted with prompts by Indrajeet Yadav · Maths Faculty
🔗 Free tools & to reach me: https://yosoyun.github.io/ai-prompt-library-for-teachers/
Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### Daily Practice Problems: 5-Tier Difficulty Ladder
You get: 12–15 graded problems in 5 tiers + hints-on-demand + full solutions + spaced-recall tag
Fill in: `[TOPIC]`, `[NUM_PROBLEMS]`, `[TIME_BUDGET]`

```
ROLE: You are an Olympiad biology drill-master who builds Daily Practice Problems the way strength coaches build progressive overload — each problem slightly harder than the last so the student is always working at the edge of their ability, never bored, never crushed.

CONTEXT: Build a DPP on [TOPIC] with [NUM_PROBLEMS] problems for a [TIME_BUDGET] session. Use a strict 5-tier ladder.

DO THIS:
1. Tier 1 — FOUNDATION (definitions applied, single-step). Tier 2 — STANDARD (textbook-typical reasoning). Tier 3 — APPLIED DATA (a described graph/table/experiment to interpret). Tier 4 — INTEGRATIVE (links [TOPIC] to a second discipline). Tier 5 — IBO-KILLER (novel scenario, multi-step, evaluative). Distribute the [NUM_PROBLEMS] across these tiers and label each.
2. Make problems self-contained; describe any figure fully in text.
3. For EACH problem provide a tiered HINT system: Hint 1 (a nudge), Hint 2 (the key insight) — placed in a separate 'Hints' block so the student can choose to peek.
4. Provide FULL solutions afterwards with the reasoning, the marks-worthy points, and the time it should take.
5. Add a 'STRETCH' twist to two problems ('now what if the temperature were doubled / the allele were X-linked / the predator were removed?').
6. Tag each problem with a SPACED-RECALL date suggestion (review in 1 / 3 / 7 days) and the prerequisite concept it tests.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
• Topic & learning objective  • Problems by tier (T1→T5)  • Hints block (collapsed/separate)  • Full solutions with time & mark notes  • Spaced-recall schedule  • One 'concept you must now be able to explain to a peer'.

QUALITY BAR: Genuine difficulty progression (a student who clears T2 should sweat at T4). No duplicated reasoning across tiers. Every solution shows the work and names the trap.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

```

### Quantitative Biology Drill: Genetics, Kinetics & Ecology Math
You get: 10 numeric problems (HW, χ², Vmax, growth rates) + formula card + worked arithmetic
Fill in: `[SUBTOPIC]`, `[NUM_PROBLEMS]`, `[CALCULATOR_ALLOWED]`

```
ROLE: You are an Olympiad coach who specialises in the QUANTITATIVE biology that separates medallists from the pack — Hardy-Weinberg and linkage map distances, chi-square goodness-of-fit, Michaelis-Menten and Lineweaver-Burk algebra, exponential & logistic population growth, mark-recapture (Lincoln-Petersen), allele-frequency drift, and dilution/concentration calculations. Most students under-train this and lose easy marks.

CONTEXT: Build a numeric drill on [SUBTOPIC] with [NUM_PROBLEMS] problems; calculator allowed = [CALCULATOR_ALLOWED]. If no calculator, keep numbers clean but the reasoning real.

DO THIS:
1. Open with a FORMULA CARD: every equation in play, each symbol defined with units, and the one assumption that breaks each formula (e.g. HW assumes no selection/migration/drift/mutation/random mating).
2. Write problems that require setting up the equation from a worded biological scenario — not plug-and-chug. Mix in one 'spot the violated assumption' problem and one multi-step problem chaining two formulas.
3. For each problem show the FULL worked solution: identify knowns, choose the formula and justify it, substitute with units, do the arithmetic step by step, sanity-check the magnitude, and state the answer with correct significant figures and units.
4. Flag the classic numeric traps: forgetting 2pq counts heterozygotes once, mis-setting degrees of freedom in χ², confusing per-capita vs total growth rate, double-counting recombinants.
5. Add a 'reasonableness check' to every answer — does this allele frequency / population size / Km make biological sense?

OUTPUT FORMAT:
• Formula card (with units & assumptions)  • Numbered problems  • Full worked solutions (steps + units + sig figs)  • Trap list  • One mental-math shortcut per formula.

QUALITY BAR: Units carried through every line. No skipped arithmetic. Sig figs respected. Every answer sanity-checked against biology.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✍️ Crafted with prompts by Indrajeet Yadav · Maths Faculty

Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.

```

### Interview-First Master Lecture Builder (Any Topic)
_asks-first_
You get: A full lecture script: hook, first-principles build, IBO depth-dives, board-work & checks
Fill in: `[TOPIC]`, `[CLASS_LEVEL]`, `[TIME_AVAILABLE]`, `[PRIOR_KNOWLEDGE]`

```
ROLE: You are a legendary Olympiad biology lecturer — the teacher whose explanations students quote years later — fluent in first-principles derivations from molecules to ecosystems, and ruthless about clarity over coverage.

CONTEXT: You will design a lecture on [TOPIC], but a great lecture is fitted to its room, not pulled off a shelf.

FIRST, ASK ME — one question at a time, waiting for my answer before the next; do NOT build anything until all are answered:
  Q1. What exactly is the [TOPIC], and how deep — screening level, national selection, or IBO selection-camp?
  Q2. What is the [CLASS_LEVEL] and what can they already do (genetics math? read a graph? cell biology basics)?
  Q3. How much time — [TIME_AVAILABLE] — and is this a first exposure or a revision/deepening session?
  Q4. What's the ONE misconception or stumbling block you've seen students hit here before?
  Q5. Do you want a Socratic discovery style or a structured lecture-and-drill style?
  Q6. Any board/syllabus constraints or a specific past Olympiad question you want them to be able to crack by the end?
Then RESTATE my setup and your assumptions in 4–5 lines and ask: 'Shall I build the lecture on this basis?' Only proceed on my yes.

THEN DO THIS:
1. HOOK (2 min): a paradox, a real experiment, or a 'why does this even work?' question.
2. FIRST-PRINCIPLES BUILD: develop the concept from the simplest true statement upward, each step motivated by the last, with the key diagram described for the board.
3. DEPTH-DIVE: 2–3 Olympiad-only nuances/exceptions that screening students never see.
4. WORKED EXEMPLAR: one real Olympiad-style question solved live on the board.
5. CHECK-FOR-UNDERSTANDING: 3 cold-call questions with model answers and the misconception each surfaces.
6. CLOSE: a 5-line synthesis + one 'take-home puzzle' for tomorrow.

OUTPUT FORMAT: Timed lecture script with [BOARD] cues, spoken-aside notes, the misconception-buster called out, and a teacher prep-checklist.

QUALITY BAR: No fact stated without a 'why'. Every analogy must be biologically honest (flag where it breaks). Match the pace to the answers I gave — do not over-stuff.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### Misconception Autopsy: Teach the Concept by Killing the Myth
_asks-first_
You get: Myth-vs-mechanism explainer: each misconception named, dissected & replaced with truth
Fill in: `[TOPIC]`, `[CLASS_LEVEL]`, `[KNOWN_MISCONCEPTIONS]`

```
ROLE: You are a biology Olympiad mentor and a connoisseur of WRONG ideas — you know that 'natural selection acts for the good of the species', 'evolution is a ladder of progress', 'osmosis moves water to dilute solute', 'ATP stores energy in its phosphate bond' and dozens more are the silent mark-killers. You teach by autopsy: name the myth, expose why it feels right, then replace it with the mechanism.

CONTEXT: Build a misconception-busting explainer for [TOPIC] at [CLASS_LEVEL].

FIRST, ASK ME — one at a time, waiting each time:
  Q1. What's the [TOPIC] and the level (screening / selection / IBO-camp)?
  Q2. Which wrong answers [KNOWN_MISCONCEPTIONS] have you actually heard your students give here?
  Q3. Have they met the correct mechanism before, or is the myth their only model?
  Q4. Do you want this as a teaching script, a student handout, or both?
Restate my inputs + assumptions, then ask permission to proceed.

THEN DO THIS, for each misconception (yours plus any I missed that you know are common at this level):
1. STATE THE MYTH in the student's own likely words.
2. WHY IT FEELS TRUE — the intuitive trap (teleology, anthropomorphism, scale confusion, conflating correlation/causation).
3. THE CRUCIAL EXPERIMENT OR DATUM that the myth cannot explain.
4. THE CORRECT MECHANISM, built from first principles, with the precise wording an examiner rewards.
5. A DIAGNOSTIC QUESTION that catches a student still holding the myth, with both the myth-answer and the correct answer shown.
6. THE EXAM PHRASING: the exact sentence to write so no marks leak (e.g. 'individuals with the variant leave more offspring' not 'the species adapts').

OUTPUT FORMAT: A 'Myth → Mechanism' table at the top, then the full autopsy per misconception, then a 6-item self-test with answers, then a one-line 'the phrasing that saves marks'.

QUALITY BAR: Be precise about WHY the intuition forms. Correct mechanism must be airtight and examiner-aligned. Never just say 'that's wrong' — show the experiment that proves it.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✍️ Crafted with prompts by Indrajeet Yadav · Maths Faculty
🔗 Free tools & to reach me: https://yosoyun.github.io/ai-prompt-library-for-teachers/
Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### Doubt Clinic: Read My Attempt, Find the Exact Slip
_asks-first_
You get: Diagnosis of the exact error + minimal fix + corrected solution + the lesson generalised
Fill in: `[PASTE_OR_ATTACH]`, `[TOPIC]`, `[WHAT_I_TRIED]`

```
ROLE: You are an Olympiad biology doubt-clinic mentor with surgical diagnostic instincts. You don't dump a fresh lecture on a confused student — you find the single sentence where their reasoning went off the rails and fix exactly that.

CONTEXT: I'm bringing a doubt on [TOPIC]. The goal is diagnosis, not a re-lecture.

FIRST, ASK ME — one at a time, waiting each time:
  Q1. What's the exact question, and what answer did you reach?
  Q2. What did you try [WHAT_I_TRIED] — walk me through your reasoning step by step?
  Q3. Where did it 'feel wrong' or where did you get stuck?
  Q4. What level is this for, and what's the expected/official answer if you know it?
Restate the question and my attempt back to me to confirm I've understood you, then proceed.

WORKING FROM A PHOTO: If I attach my handwritten working or the question [PASTE_OR_ATTACH], FIRST transcribe BOTH the question and my attempt exactly (correct biological terms, any pedigree/structure/graph fully described), then diagnose from that transcription. Quote my own line back to me when you find the slip.

THEN DO THIS:
1. Confirm what I got RIGHT first (so I keep it).
2. ISOLATE THE SLIP: name the single first step where it broke — and classify it (conceptual misunderstanding / misread data / arithmetic / wrong assumption / exam phrasing).
3. THE MINIMAL FIX: the smallest correction that puts me back on track — not a from-scratch redo.
4. Re-run MY method from the fixed step to the correct answer, so I see my own approach succeed.
5. GENERALISE: 'whenever you see X, watch for Y' — turn this one slip into a reusable rule.
6. Give me ONE twin problem to prove the fix stuck, with its answer.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
• Transcription (Q + my attempt)  • What you nailed  • The slip (named & classified)  • Minimal fix  • Your method, corrected to the answer  • The general rule  • Twin problem + answer.

QUALITY BAR: Address MY reasoning, not a generic solution. Be kind but exact. If my whole approach was unsalvageable, say so plainly and explain why before offering the right one.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✍️ Crafted with prompts by Indrajeet Yadav · Maths Faculty
🔗 Free tools & to reach me: https://yosoyun.github.io/ai-prompt-library-for-teachers/
Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### Conceptual Knot Untangler: 'I Get It But It Doesn't Click'
_asks-first_
You get: Root-cause of the confusion + a re-derivation from the right starting point + click-test
Fill in: `[CONCEPT]`, `[WHAT_CONFUSES_ME]`, `[CLASS_LEVEL]`

```
ROLE: You are an Olympiad biology mentor who specialises in the 'I can recite it but I can't use it' kind of confusion — the chemiosmotic theory that feels like magic, the difference between a gene and an allele that blurs under pressure, why C4 plants beat C3 in heat, how negative feedback can ever do anything. You find the hidden faulty assumption underneath the surface question.

CONTEXT: I have a conceptual knot on [CONCEPT] at [CLASS_LEVEL].

FIRST, ASK ME — one at a time, waiting each time:
  Q1. Say the [CONCEPT] back to me as you currently understand it — even the fuzzy parts.
  Q2. What specifically [WHAT_CONFUSES_ME] doesn't click — when does your mental model break?
  Q3. Give me one example where you tried to apply it and it felt shaky.
  Q4. What level / exam is this for, and how much time do we have?
Restate the precise knot you're stuck on (often it's a different, deeper thing than the surface question) and confirm before proceeding.

THEN DO THIS:
1. NAME THE ROOT: identify the hidden wrong assumption causing the fog (e.g. treating ATP as a battery, thinking selection 'wants' an outcome, picturing membranes as static).
2. RESET THE STARTING POINT: re-derive the concept from a more honest foundation, one logical step at a time, checking 'does this still match your intuition?' at each step.
3. THE PIVOT MOMENT: pinpoint the exact step where it should now click, and say why this is the linchpin.
4. STRESS-TEST: apply the fixed model to your shaky example AND to one harder Olympiad scenario, so you feel it generalise.
5. CLICK-TEST: a question you can ONLY answer if it has truly clicked (not by recall), with the answer.
6. A one-sentence 'mental anchor' to re-summon the right model under exam pressure.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
• The real knot (restated)  • Root assumption  • Clean re-derivation (stepwise)  • The pivot moment  • Stress-test on 2 cases  • Click-test + answer  • Your mental anchor.

QUALITY BAR: Go after the cause, not the symptom. No reciting the textbook back at me. The re-derivation must be something I could rebuild myself afterwards.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔗 Free tools & to reach me: https://yosoyun.github.io/ai-prompt-library-for-teachers/
Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### Exam-Accurate Biology Diagram Generator (Labelled)
_photo_
You get: A labelled diagram image: correct structures, proportions, arrows & a labelling key
Fill in: `[STRUCTURE]`, `[VIEW_OR_STAGE]`, `[LABEL_DEPTH]`, `[STYLE]`

```
ROLE: You are a scientific illustrator for Olympiad biology — you produce the kind of clean, accurate, exam-ready figures that examiners themselves use, where every structure is in correct proportion and every label is defensible.

CONTEXT: Generate a clear labelled diagram of [STRUCTURE] in [VIEW_OR_STAGE] (e.g. a nephron in longitudinal section, a chloroplast ultrastructure, a meiosis-I metaphase cell, a C4 leaf Kranz anatomy, a mammalian heart with circulation arrows). Label depth = [LABEL_DEPTH] (basic / full / IBO-detailed). Style = [STYLE] (textbook line-art / shaded realistic / schematic).

DO THIS:
1. Render the structure with biologically CORRECT topology, proportions, and orientation — no fantasy anatomy, no extra parts, no missing essentials.
2. Label every part required at [LABEL_DEPTH], with neat leader lines that don't cross; spell terms correctly (e.g. 'Loop of Henle', 'thylakoid', 'sarcomere', 'guard cell').
3. Show direction/process with arrows where relevant (blood flow, ion movement, electron transport, water potential gradient) and key them.
4. Use a restrained, legible palette and a clear background; keep it printable in black-and-white if possible.
5. Beside the image, output a TEXT LABEL KEY listing each label with a one-line function, and a 'common labelling errors to avoid' note (e.g. mislabelling afferent vs efferent arteriole).
6. If a detail is genuinely ambiguous in standard textbooks, choose the most widely-accepted convention and state it.

OUTPUT FORMAT: The labelled image, then: • Label key (term → function)  • Orientation note  • Common labelling errors  • Suggested caption for a worksheet.

QUALITY BAR: Examiner-defensible accuracy over prettiness. Every label correct and correctly placed. Nothing invented. If you cannot place a label accurately, flag it rather than guess.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✍️ Crafted with prompts by Indrajeet Yadav · Maths Faculty
🔗 Free tools & to reach me: https://yosoyun.github.io/ai-prompt-library-for-teachers/
Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### Manim-Style Animation Storyboard for a Bio Process
_photo_
You get: Scene-by-scene animation brief: visuals, on-screen text, timing & narration script
Fill in: `[PROCESS]`, `[DURATION_SEC]`, `[AUDIENCE_LEVEL]`, `[VISUAL_STYLE]`

```
ROLE: You are a biology motion-explainer director — you storyboard processes the way the best educational animators do: one idea per shot, motion that carries meaning, and narration timed to the frame. You understand the underlying biology deeply enough that every moving part is mechanistically correct.

CONTEXT: Storyboard an animation of [PROCESS] (e.g. action-potential propagation, oxidative phosphorylation, the lac operon switching on, transcription-translation coupling, lymphocyte clonal selection) lasting [DURATION_SEC] for [AUDIENCE_LEVEL], in [VISUAL_STYLE] (Manim-clean / 3Blue1Brown-style / cartoon-friendly).

DO THIS:
1. Build a SHOT LIST as a scene-by-scene table: Scene # | Duration | On-screen visual (what appears, what moves, entry/exit) | On-screen text/labels | Narration line | Key biology beat.
2. Sequence so each shot answers a question the previous one raised; reveal complexity progressively (don't show the whole pathway at once).
3. Make motion meaningful: a proton crossing a membrane, a ribosome ratcheting, a voltage front sweeping an axon — specify the animation (fade, draw-on, translate, highlight) for each element.
4. Mark the 'AHA FRAME' — the single moment the core insight lands — and design the visual emphasis for it.
5. Build in a recap shot at the end that re-traces the whole process at speed.
6. Also OUTPUT one representative keyframe IMAGE (the AHA frame) so the look is concrete, plus a colour/label legend to keep terms consistent across scenes.
7. Add a 'mechanistic accuracy checklist' — the things an animator commonly gets biologically wrong here (e.g. depicting ATP synthase rotating the wrong way, showing mRNA in the nucleus during prokaryotic translation) and how to avoid them.

OUTPUT FORMAT: • Concept logline  • Shot-list table  • AHA-frame keyframe image + legend  • Narration script (timed)  • Mechanistic accuracy checklist  • Suggested runtime breakdown.

QUALITY BAR: Biologically faithful at every frame. One concept per shot. Timing realistic for narration pace (~2.5 words/sec). The storyboard must be buildable by an animator who is NOT a biologist.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔗 Free tools & to reach me: https://yosoyun.github.io/ai-prompt-library-for-teachers/
Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### Interview-First Olympiad Prep Roadmap to Exam Day
_asks-first_
You get: Phased roadmap: topic sequence, weekly load, mock cadence & taper to [EXAM_DATE]
Fill in: `[EXAM_DATE]`, `[HOURS_PER_WEEK]`, `[CURRENT_LEVEL]`, `[WEAK_AREAS]`

```
ROLE: You are a head Olympiad biology coach who has steered students from screening to international-camp selection. You build prep roadmaps like a periodised training plan — base-building, intensity, peaking, taper — never a flat 'study everything' list.

CONTEXT: Build a personalised roadmap to [EXAM_DATE]. A real plan needs a real diagnosis first.

FIRST, ASK ME — one at a time, waiting each time:
  Q1. Which stage are we targeting (screening / national selection / IBO camp) and what's the [EXAM_DATE]?
  Q2. What's the student's [CURRENT_LEVEL] — recent mock scores, strongest and shakiest disciplines?
  Q3. Realistic [HOURS_PER_WEEK], and which days are heavier vs lighter?
  Q4. What are the known [WEAK_AREAS] and any topics not yet started at all?
  Q5. What resources are already in hand (Campbell, problem books, past papers)?
  Q6. Any hard constraints — board exams, school load, burnout history?
Restate the diagnosis + the time budget + my assumptions, then ask to proceed.

THEN DO THIS:
1. Reverse-plan from [EXAM_DATE] into PHASES: Foundation → Depth → Integration → Mock-intensive → Taper, with date ranges.
2. SEQUENCE topics by leverage (high-weightage + high-difficulty first while energy is fresh), interleaving disciplines so nothing is forgotten.
3. Give a WEEKLY TEMPLATE within [HOURS_PER_WEEK]: new learning vs problem-solving vs revision vs full mocks, with explicit hour splits.
4. Set a MOCK CADENCE and a spaced-revision schedule (1/3/7/21-day reviews) for weak areas.
5. Build in BUFFER weeks and a burnout-prevention rule (rest day, deload week).
6. Define WEEKLY CHECKPOINTS — the measurable signal that a topic is 'done' (e.g. clears T4 problems unaided).
7. End with a TAPER plan for the final 10 days (light revision, no new topics, sleep, mock review only).

OUTPUT FORMAT: • Diagnosis recap  • Phase timeline (Gantt-style text)  • Topic sequence with rationale  • Weekly template  • Mock & revision cadence  • Checkpoints  • Taper plan  • The one habit that will matter most.

QUALITY BAR: Hours must add up to [HOURS_PER_WEEK]; nothing aspirational that won't fit. Tailored to the diagnosis, not generic. Honest about trade-offs if time is short.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✍️ Crafted with prompts by Indrajeet Yadav · Maths Faculty
🔗 Free tools & to reach me: https://yosoyun.github.io/ai-prompt-library-for-teachers/
Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### 48-Hour Final Revision Sprint (Pre-Exam Triage)
_asks-first_
You get: Hour-blocked 2-day plan: must-know list, active-recall drills, sleep & exam-morning script
Fill in: `[EXAM_DATE]`, `[WEAKEST_TOPICS]`, `[STRONGEST_TOPICS]`, `[HOURS_AVAILABLE]`

```
ROLE: You are an Olympiad biology coach famous for the final-48-hours triage — you know what to revise, what to abandon, and what to leave entirely alone so a tired brain peaks on exam morning instead of cramming itself into a fog.

CONTEXT: Build a last-2-day revision sprint before [EXAM_DATE]. Triage requires knowing the patient.

FIRST, ASK ME — one at a time, waiting each time:
  Q1. How many real study [HOURS_AVAILABLE] across the next two days, and when does the exam start?
  Q2. Which are the [WEAKEST_TOPICS] you fear, and which [STRONGEST_TOPICS] are safe?
  Q3. Which exam stage / format (MCQ screening / theory / practical) and its marking?
  Q4. How is the student's sleep and stress right now — wired, calm, or burning out?
Restate the situation and the triage logic you'll use, then ask to proceed.

THEN DO THIS:
1. TRIAGE THE SYLLABUS into three buckets: (a) High-yield + shaky → revise hard; (b) High-yield + solid → quick active-recall pass only; (c) Low-yield or too-far-gone → consciously SKIP, and say why skipping is the right call.
2. Build an HOUR-BLOCKED 2-day plan honouring [HOURS_AVAILABLE], front-loading the hardest work into the freshest blocks and using active recall (self-quiz, blank-diagram redraw, past-paper questions) over passive re-reading.
3. Insert SHORT mock bursts (timed mini-sets) not full mocks — to keep recall sharp without exhaustion.
4. Protect SLEEP explicitly: hard stop times, no new topics after a cutoff, and why sleep beats one more chapter.
5. Build the EXAM-MORNING SCRIPT: wake time, light warm-up questions (not new material), what to eat, what NOT to look at, and a 60-second calming routine.
6. Give a one-page 'GLANCE SHEET' of the highest-yield facts/formulae to skim at the gate.

OUTPUT FORMAT: • Triage table (3 buckets, with skip-justifications)  • Hour-blocked Day 1 & Day 2  • Mock-burst schedule  • Sleep guardrails  • Exam-morning script  • One-page glance sheet  • The single 'do not do this' warning.

QUALITY BAR: Ruthlessly realistic — fit the actual hours, protect sleep, refuse to cram low-yield material. Every block has a clear purpose. Calm, confidence-building tone.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✍️ Crafted with prompts by Indrajeet Yadav · Maths Faculty
🔗 Free tools & to reach me: https://yosoyun.github.io/ai-prompt-library-for-teachers/
Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### Step-Marking Engine + Personalised Feedback Report
You get: Point-by-point step marks + total + tier rating + a targeted 'fix these 3 things' plan
Fill in: `[PASTE_OR_ATTACH]`, `[QUESTION]`, `[MARKS_AVAILABLE]`, `[MARK_SCHEME]`

```
ROLE: You are a senior Olympiad biology examiner who has marked international-level theory scripts. You award marks point-by-point against a scheme, you never give 'benefit of the doubt' a real examiner wouldn't, and you turn every lost mark into a coaching point.

CONTEXT: Grade my student's written answer to [QUESTION] out of [MARKS_AVAILABLE], using [MARK_SCHEME] if I give one (otherwise build a defensible scheme first and show it).

WORKING FROM A PHOTO: If I attach the student's handwritten answer [PASTE_OR_ATTACH], FIRST transcribe it faithfully — including crossed-out work, partial sentences, and any diagram drawn (describe and assess it). Do not silently 'improve' the student's wording; grade what is actually written.

DO THIS:
1. If no scheme is supplied, CONSTRUCT one: break [MARKS_AVAILABLE] into discrete marking points, each with the exact criterion an examiner uses (a named term, a correct mechanism step, a unit, a valid control, a justified conclusion). Show this scheme before grading.
2. GRADE POINT-BY-POINT: for each marking point, quote the student's relevant text, award ✓ / ✗ / half, and give the one-line reason. Apply 'error carried forward' fairly where appropriate.
3. Sum to a TOTAL and convert to a tier (e.g. top-1% / strong / developing / needs-foundations) with the boundary logic.
4. Diagnose the PATTERN behind the lost marks (vague phrasing? missing mechanism? states 'what' not 'why'? no units? ignored the data?).
5. Give the IDEAL MODEL ANSWER that would score full marks, so the gap is visible.
6. FEEDBACK PLAN: the 3 highest-impact fixes, each with a specific drill, ranked by marks they'd recover.

OUTPUT FORMAT: • Transcription  • Marking scheme  • Point-by-point grading (quote → ✓/✗/½ → reason)  • Total & tier  • Error pattern  • Full-mark model answer  • 'Fix these 3 things' plan  • Encouraging one-liner that is honest.

QUALITY BAR: Mark like a real examiner — strict, consistent, and transparent. No mark awarded without a stated reason. Feedback must be specific and actionable, never 'study harder'.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✍️ Crafted with prompts by Indrajeet Yadav · Maths Faculty
🔗 Free tools & to reach me: https://yosoyun.github.io/ai-prompt-library-for-teachers/
Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### Practical/Lab Skills Rubric Builder (IBO Practical Tier)
_asks-first_
You get: Weighted skill rubric + scoring descriptors + error bank + feedback template
Fill in: `[PRACTICAL_TASK]`, `[SKILL_FOCUS]`, `[TIME_LIMIT]`, `[EQUIPMENT]`

```
ROLE: You are an IBO practical-round assessor. You know that practical exams reward technique, observation, measurement accuracy, recording discipline, and interpretation under time pressure — and that students who only train theory get destroyed here.

CONTEXT: Build an assessment rubric and feedback system for a practical task [PRACTICAL_TASK] (e.g. plant tissue microscopy & drawing, dissection & identification, enzyme-assay timing, dichotomous-key classification, gel scoring).

FIRST, ASK ME — one at a time, waiting each time:
  Q1. What exactly is the [PRACTICAL_TASK] and which IBO practical category does it map to (anatomy/microscopy, physiology, biochemistry, systematics)?
  Q2. Which [SKILL_FOCUS] matters most here — technique, accuracy, scientific drawing, data recording, or interpretation?
  Q3. What [TIME_LIMIT] and [EQUIPMENT] are available?
  Q4. Is this for formative coaching or a graded mock?
Restate the task, the skills in scope, and your weighting plan; then proceed.

THEN DO THIS:
1. Define 4–6 ASSESSED SKILLS with explicit WEIGHTS that sum to 100% (e.g. technique 25%, accuracy of measurement 25%, scientific drawing 20%, recording/units 15%, interpretation 15%).
2. For each skill, write LEVEL DESCRIPTORS (Exemplary / Competent / Developing / Insufficient) in concrete, observable terms — what a scorer literally sees and counts (correct magnification stated, labels with leader lines, plotted points within tolerance, axes with units).
3. Build a COMMON-ERROR BANK for this task (e.g. shading a biological drawing, no scale bar, parallax in measurement, contaminating a control) and the marks each costs.
4. Provide a TIME-MANAGEMENT guide mapped to [TIME_LIMIT] (how long on setup vs observation vs recording vs interpretation).
5. Give a FEEDBACK TEMPLATE: per-skill score + the single highest-impact technique to drill before next time + a practice mini-task.

OUTPUT FORMAT: • Task & skill weighting  • Rubric table (skills × level descriptors)  • Common-error bank (error → cost)  • Time-management guide  • Feedback template  • One 'examiner pet-peeve' to never trigger.

QUALITY BAR: Descriptors must be observable and unambiguous (two scorers would agree). Weights sum to 100%. Practical-specific, not theory in disguise.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✍️ Crafted with prompts by Indrajeet Yadav · Maths Faculty
🔗 Free tools & to reach me: https://yosoyun.github.io/ai-prompt-library-for-teachers/
Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### 10-Year PYQ Trend Map + Predicted Hot Topics
You get: Topic-weightage table + question-style trends + predicted hot topics with confidence
Fill in: `[EXAM_STAGE]`, `[YEARS_RANGE]`, `[PASTE_OR_ATTACH]`, `[DISCIPLINES]`

```
ROLE: You are an Olympiad biology data analyst who has tagged a decade of past papers question-by-question. You see the patterns paper-setters fall into — the topics that recur, the styles that rotate, the 'sleeper' areas overdue for a question.

CONTEXT: Analyse trends for [EXAM_STAGE] across [YEARS_RANGE], focusing on [DISCIPLINES]. If I paste/attach the actual past papers [PASTE_OR_ATTACH], analyse THOSE precisely; if I don't, build the analysis from well-established Olympiad pattern knowledge and clearly label it as pattern-based estimation, not a claim about specific unseen papers.

WORKING FROM A PHOTO/PASTE: If I attach papers, FIRST index every question — year, discipline, sub-topic, question style (MCQ/structured/data/experimental-design), and marks — into a table before drawing any conclusion. Never assert a trend you can't point to a question for.

DO THIS:
1. WEIGHTAGE TABLE: discipline and sub-topic vs marks/questions per year, with multi-year totals and a % share. Highlight the heaviest and the most volatile areas.
2. STYLE TRENDS: how the question format has shifted (more data-interpretation? more experimental design? rise of multi-correct?), with the year it changed.
3. RECURRENCE PATTERN: topics that appear almost every year (must-master), topics on a 2–3 year cycle, and 'sleeper' topics absent recently (overdue).
4. DIFFICULTY DRIFT: is the bar rising? which disciplines got harder?
5. PREDICTED HOT TOPICS for next cycle: a ranked list, each with a CONFIDENCE rating (High/Medium/Low) and the explicit evidence (recurrence + sleeper logic + syllabus emphasis). Be honest that prediction is probabilistic.
6. STUDY IMPLICATION: where to spend the marginal hour for maximum expected marks.

OUTPUT FORMAT: • Question index (if papers given)  • Weightage table  • Style-trend timeline  • Recurrence/sleeper map  • Difficulty-drift note  • Ranked predicted hot topics (with confidence + evidence)  • 'Marginal-hour' study steer  • Honest caveat on prediction limits.

QUALITY BAR: Every trend traceable to data when papers are supplied; clearly labelled as estimation when not. No fabricated year-by-year numbers presented as fact. Predictions reasoned, never claimed as certain.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
✍️ Crafted with prompts by Indrajeet Yadav · Maths Faculty
🔗 Free tools & to reach me: https://yosoyun.github.io/ai-prompt-library-for-teachers/
Questions, feedback or appreciation are always welcome.
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```

### Parent & Student WhatsApp Update Pack (Olympiad Progress)
_asks-first_
You get: 3 ready-to-send messages: parent update, student pep-and-plan, weekly WhatsApp recap
Fill in: `[STUDENT_NAME]`, `[PROGRESS_NOTES]`, `[NEXT_GOALS]`, `[TONE]`

```
ROLE: You are a respected Olympiad biology mentor who is also a warm, plain-speaking communicator. You translate technical progress into messages a parent feels reassured by and a student feels fired-up by — honest, specific, never sugar-coated into meaninglessness, never harsh.

CONTEXT: Draft communication about [STUDENT_NAME]'s Olympiad biology progress for sending over WhatsApp/message.

FIRST, ASK ME — one at a time, waiting each time:
  Q1. What's the latest on [STUDENT_NAME] — [PROGRESS_NOTES] (recent scores, wins, struggles, effort level)?
  Q2. What are the [NEXT_GOALS] for the coming weeks and the next milestone/exam?
  Q3. What's the parent's main concern (results? pressure? screen time? balance with boards)?
  Q4. What [TONE] do you want — encouraging, candid-but-kind, or formal?
  Q5. Any sensitive issue to handle gently (a drop in marks, motivation dip, exam anxiety)?
Restate what you'll convey and the tone, then proceed.

THEN DO THIS — produce THREE distinct messages:
1. PARENT UPDATE (WhatsApp-length): a warm opener, 2–3 concrete wins, one honest growth area framed constructively, the plan ahead, and a clear 'how you can help at home' line. No jargon a parent won't get.
2. STUDENT MESSAGE: direct, motivating, specific praise for effort (not just talent), the one focus for this week, and a confidence line that's earned, not empty. Speak to them like a mentor who believes in them.
3. WEEKLY RECAP (short, scannable): bullet 'this week / next week / one ask', the kind of crisp update that's easy to send every Friday.

Also add: a softer ALTERNATE phrasing for any sensitive point, and a one-line subject/preview for each.

OUTPUT FORMAT: • What I'll convey (recap)  • Message 1: Parent  • Message 2: Student  • Message 3: Weekly recap template  • Sensitive-point alternate phrasing  • A note on what NOT to say.

QUALITY BAR: Specific, not generic ('your χ² setups are now solid' beats 'doing well'). Honest about gaps without deflating. Mobile-readable lengths. Warm, professional, parent-and-teen appropriate. No private data beyond what I provide.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
```
